sections.translationMarkdown: LSM-tree 是用于键值存储的一种持久化多级索引结构，其目标是将代价高昂的原地更新转换为顺序更新，从而获得高效的写性能。所有 update、insert 和 delete 最初都会被转换为键值 entry，随后在主存缓冲区（或 MemTable）中排序。当 MemTable 填满时，它会作为一个新的 sorted run（在 RocksDB 中称为 SSTable，简称 SST）flush 到磁盘。这些 SST 被组织在多个 level 中，每个 level 的容量都是前一 level 的 $T$ 倍。在采用典型 Leveling compaction 策略的 LSM-tree 中，例如 Pebble [52]，同一 level 中的 SST 互不重叠，共同组成一个 sorted run。相比之下，ScyllaDB [80] 等基于 Tiering 的系统允许每个 level 最多保留 $T$ 个 key 范围相互重叠的 sorted run，从而减小 compaction 规模并改善写性能。基于 LSM 的系统通常支持三种基本操作，如图 1 所示：

sections.title: 2.1 LSM-tree
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Figure 1: How the three basic operations, point lookup, range lookup, and update, are performed in an LSM-tree system.**

**Point Lookup.** Given a key, the key-value store returns its associated value if it exists. The search proceeds by scanning each sorted run sequentially, stopping once the value is found. This process relies on the LSM-tree’s timestamp ordering across levels: the smallest timestamp in the $i$-th level must be no smaller than the largest timestamp in the $(i-1)$-th level. Within the same level, runs may have overlapping timestamps. If multiple versions of a key exist at a given level, the system returns the most recent one based on timestamp comparison. Without this cross-level timestamp order, point lookups would require searching all levels for every query, severely degrading performance. Each sorted run is equipped with a Bloom filter, an in-memory structure that quickly determines whether a key may exist in the run. Its accuracy is controlled by the bits-per-key (BPK) parameter, representing the ratio of filter memory to the number of keys. The false positive rate (FPR) follows $FPR = O(e^{-BPK(\ln 2)^2})$. Let $s$ be the total number of sorted runs; the I/O cost of a point lookup is then $O(s \cdot FPR + 1)$.

**Range Lookup.** Different from point lookup, the LSM-tree retrieves all the entries within a specified key range from all the sorted runs. And then it sort merges the results from each sorted runs and produces a final result. Specifically, as most LSM-tree systems leverage iterator to iteratively produce the final result, which reads the first data block (usually sized one I/O block) from each sorted run and then fetches the entries one by one from each sorted runs. Suppose the search range contains $l$ entries, each of size $E$ bytes, and the I/O block size is $B$ bytes, the I/O cost is $O(s + \frac{lE}{B})$.

**Update.** In an LSM-tree, new key-value pairs are first inserted into an in-memory buffer called the MemTable. Once the MemTable reaches its threshold size, it is flushed to disk as a new sorted run. Updates to existing keys are handled using the same out-of-place insertion mechanism, appending the new version without modifying prior entries. When the size of a level exceeds its predefined capacity, a compaction is triggered to merge its sorted runs with those in the next level.

Modern LSM-tree key-value systems execute queries and updates on foreground threads, while use background threads to asynchronously handle the flush and compaction when the MemTable or levels become full.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**图 1：LSM-tree 系统执行点查询、范围查询和更新这三种基本操作的方式。**

**点查询（Point Lookup）。** 给定一个 key，如果其关联的 value 存在，键值存储就返回该 value。搜索过程依次扫描各个 sorted run，并在找到 value 后停止。该过程依赖 LSM-tree 跨 level 的时间戳顺序：第 $i$ 个 level 中的最小时间戳不得小于第 $(i-1)$ 个 level 中的最大时间戳。同一 level 内的 run 可以具有相互重叠的时间戳。如果某个 level 中存在同一 key 的多个版本，系统会通过比较时间戳返回最新版本。若不存在这种跨 level 的时间戳顺序，每次点查询都必须搜索所有 level，性能将严重下降。每个 sorted run 都配有 Bloom filter；它是一种驻留在内存中的结构，可以快速判断某个 key 是否可能存在于该 run 中。Bloom filter 的准确率由 bits-per-key（BPK）参数控制，该参数表示 filter 内存与 key 数量之比。假阳性率（FPR）满足 $FPR = O(e^{-BPK(\ln 2)^2})$。设 sorted run 的总数为 $s$，则点查询的 I/O 开销为 $O(s \cdot FPR + 1)$。

**范围查询（Range Lookup）。** 与点查询不同，LSM-tree 会从所有 sorted run 中取回指定 key range 内的全部 entry，然后对各 sorted run 的结果执行排序归并，生成最终结果。具体而言，大多数 LSM-tree 系统利用 iterator 迭代地产生最终结果：它首先从每个 sorted run 中读取第一个 data block（其大小通常为一个 I/O block），然后逐个从各 sorted run 中取出 entry。假设查询范围包含 $l$ 个 entry，每个 entry 的大小为 $E$ 字节，I/O block 大小为 $B$ 字节，则 I/O 开销为 $O(s + \frac{lE}{B})$。

**更新（Update）。** 在 LSM-tree 中，新的键值对首先被插入名为 MemTable 的内存缓冲区。当 MemTable 达到其大小阈值后，它会作为新的 sorted run 被 flush 到磁盘。对现有 key 的 update 采用相同的非原地插入机制：追加新版本而不修改原有 entry。当某个 level 的大小超过其预定义容量时，系统会触发 compaction，将其中的 sorted run 与下一 level 中的 run 合并。

现代 LSM-tree 键值系统使用前台线程执行 query 和 update，同时使用后台线程在 MemTable 或 level 填满时异步处理 flush 和 compaction。

sections.title: 2.2 Write Stall Controller
sections.originalMarkdown: The write stall controller is a critical component in most LSM-tree-based storage systems, including RocksDB [33], Pebble [52], Cassandra [53], and InfluxDB [47]. It controls the number of sorted runs at the first level (L0) by deliberately stalling incoming writes when they exceed a configurable threshold to maintain a designated number of sorted runs in the system. When a stall is triggered, the new incoming update will be forced to wait for several microseconds. Existing workload-aware methods [26, 27, 58, 64] stall writes when the number of sorted runs in the first level (L0) exceeds the predefined maximum in the structural configuration.
sections.translationMarkdown: 写入停顿控制器是大多数基于 LSM-tree 的存储系统中的关键组件，包括 RocksDB [33]、Pebble [52]、Cassandra [53] 和 InfluxDB [47]。它控制第一层（L0）的 sorted run 数量：当该数量超过可配置阈值时，控制器会有意暂停新到达的写入，从而将系统中的 sorted run 维持在指定数量。当触发停顿时，新到达的 update 会被迫等待数微秒。现有 workload-aware 方法 [26, 27, 58, 64] 会在第一层（L0）的 sorted run 数量超过结构配置中预定义的最大值时暂停写入。

sections.title: 2.3 Open Challenges
sections.originalMarkdown:
Existing approaches such as Wacky, Moose, Dostoevsky, and Ruskey can derive effective LSM configurations for static workloads, but struggle to transition between configurations with both low cost and high responsiveness. As illustrated in Figure 2, a read-intensive workload (90% reads) favors reducing the number of sorted runs from 10 to 1. A greedy transition adapts quickly but incurs high overhead by stalling writes when $K_1 = 1$. In contrast, the lazy strategy and Ruskey defer L0 adjustments, reducing transition cost but causing prolonged performance degradation before convergence.

This trade-off stems from transition mechanisms that focus only on reshaping the structure, rather than maintaining performance throughout the transition. Although a configuration may be optimal for a given workload, existing methods do not optimize system behavior during the transition itself. We argue that **sustaining optimal performance under dynamic workloads requires continuously adapting actions to the current workload and system state.**
sections.translationMarkdown:
Wacky、Moose、Dostoevsky 和 Ruskey 等现有方法能够为静态工作负载推导出有效的 LSM 配置，但难以同时以低开销和高响应速度在不同配置之间完成转换。如图 2 所示，对于读密集型工作负载（90% 为 read），将 sorted run 数量从 10 减少到 1 更为有利。贪心转换能够快速完成适应，但会在 $K_1 = 1$ 时暂停写入，从而产生较高开销。相比之下，惰性策略和 Ruskey 会推迟对 L0 的调整，虽然降低了转换开销，却会在收敛之前造成长时间的性能下降。

这种权衡源于转换机制只关注重塑结构，而没有在整个转换过程中维持性能。尽管某种配置对于给定工作负载可能是最优的，但现有方法并不会优化转换过程本身的系统行为。我们认为，**要在动态工作负载下维持最优性能，就必须根据当前工作负载和系统状态持续调整动作。**

sections.title: 3 ARCE: ADAPTIVE COMPACTION DECISION
sections.originalMarkdown:
To achieve this, we first decouple compaction behavior from rigid structural configuration parameters, such as fixed level capacities and prescribed run counts, which traditionally enforce a static and inflexible compaction schedule. We then introduce ElasticLSM, an enhanced LSM-tree design that enables the system to compact runs selected across levels and initiate compactions at workload-dependent timing, yielding two principal benefits:

- **Flexible Run Selection:** Merging sorted runs across multiple levels into one run in a single compaction improves responsiveness to read-intensive workloads and helps reduce write amplification. Also, selectively merging runs within a single level during write-intensive workloads reduces compaction overhead while slightly improving read performance.
- **Workload Dependent Timing:** By permitting compaction to be scheduled dynamically in response to workload conditions, the system can delay or advance compactions and write stalls as needed. This flexibility improves adaptability to workload shifts and mitigates the risk of performance bottlenecks under write-heavy scenarios.

For example, as shown in Figure 2, by removing structural configuration parameters, ElasticLSM can compact all runs across levels in a single operation while simultaneously raising the write stall threshold. This combination avoids transition costs and delivers even better responsiveness than the Greedy approach.

In the following, we first describe how to identify action candidates after removing parameters (Section §3.1), then present a theoretical model of system cost under this setting (Section §3.2) to guide Arce in selecting the most suitable actions over time (Section §3.3).

**Figure 2:** The example illustrates how existing structural transition policies: Greedy, Lazy, and Moderate (Ruskey), perform during and after the transition, compared with ElasticLSM’s continuous optimization approach. “Responsiveness” denotes the speed at which each method completes the transition. Performance ratings are denoted as follows: ✗ = worst, ✓ˣ = mediocre, ✓ = good, and ✓✓ = best.
sections.translationMarkdown:
为实现这一目标，我们首先将 compaction 行为与刚性的结构配置参数解耦，例如固定的层容量和预先规定的 run 数量；传统上，这些参数会强制采用静态且缺乏灵活性的 compaction 调度。随后，我们提出 ElasticLSM，这是一种增强的 LSM-tree 设计，使系统能够对跨层选取的 run 执行 compaction，并在由工作负载决定的时机启动 compaction，从而带来两项主要优势：

- **灵活的 Run 选择：** 在一次 compaction 中，将跨越多个层的 sorted run 合并为一个 run，可以提高系统对读密集型工作负载的响应速度，并有助于降低写放大。此外，在写密集型工作负载下，有选择地合并同一层内的 run，可以降低 compaction 开销，同时略微改善读性能。
- **依赖工作负载的时机：** 允许根据工作负载状况动态调度 compaction，使系统能够按需推迟或提前 compaction 与 write stall。这种灵活性提高了系统对工作负载变化的适应能力，并降低了写密集场景下出现性能瓶颈的风险。

例如，如图 2 所示，在移除结构配置参数后，ElasticLSM 可以在一次操作中 compact 跨层的所有 run，同时提高 write stall 阈值。这种组合避免了转换成本，并且响应速度甚至优于 Greedy 方法。

下文首先介绍移除参数后如何识别候选动作（第 §3.1 节），接着给出这种设置下的系统成本理论模型（第 §3.2 节），以指导 Arce 随时间选择最合适的动作（第 §3.3 节）。

**图 2：** 该示例说明了现有结构转换策略 Greedy、Lazy 和 Moderate（Ruskey）在转换期间及转换完成后的表现，并将它们与 ElasticLSM 的持续优化方法进行比较。“响应速度”表示各方法完成转换的速度。性能评级表示如下：✗ = 最差，✓ˣ = 一般，✓ = 良好，✓✓ = 最佳。

sections.title: 3.1 ElasticLSM: Expanded Action Space
sections.originalMarkdown:
ElasticLSM maintains a collection of sorted runs across levels, each potentially varying in size and count. Without fixed structural parameters on level capacities or maximum run counts, the system must explicitly decide when and how to perform its two core management actions: compaction and write stall. Write stall in ElasticLSM is straightforward: updates are throttled only when the total number of sorted runs exceeds a tunable threshold $c$, with a stalling rate $k$. This flexibility allows the system to better balance read and write throughput. Both parameters can be tuned independently, as detailed in Section §3.4. In the following, we elaborate the more complex action – compaction.

**Extensive Compaction Options.** Any level can contain an arbitrary number of sorted runs of varying sizes after removing structural constraints like level capacities, sorted runs number, and run sizes. However, this flexibility does not imply that we can freely merge any subset of runs. The core requirement of an LSM-tree is to maintain timestamp ordering across levels: timestamp at shallower level must be larger than that in deeper levels, while within the same level, sorted runs can have overlapping timestamps (see Section §2). As shown in Figure 4, within the same level, the timestamp ranges of runs can be overlapped while the bottom level must have disjoint and smaller timestamps compared to the top level. Additionally, we restrict compactions to proceed downward, following the LSM-tree tradition, to avoid complicating the timestamp order of runs within a level.

Based on these rules, we identify three compaction patterns that produce valid compaction candidate set:

- **Pattern 1 (Intra-level):** Compact any more than one sorted runs at $i$-th level, and place the result to the $i$-th level.
- **Pattern 2 (Adjacent-level):** Compact all the sorted runs from the $i$-th level with zero or more sorted runs at the $(i+1)$-th level, and place the result to the $(i+1)$-th level.
- **Pattern 3 (Multi-level):** Compact all sorted runs from the $i$-th to the $j$-th level ($j>i+1$) with zero or more sorted runs at the $(j+1)$-th level and place the result at the $(j+1)$-th level.

In general, Pattern 1 enables intra-level compaction, Pattern 2 performs traditional adjacent-level compaction, and Pattern 3 supports multi-level compaction. While these patterns enable a wide range of compaction candidates, the resulting candidate set can be extremely large and computationally expensive to process exhaustively. To address this, we apply heuristic pruning. Our observation is that, for similar sizes of compacted data, reducing a greater number of sorted runs generally yields better lookup performance. Therefore, for Pattern 1, instead of enumerating all possible combinations of runs within a level, we first sort the runs by sizes in ascending order. We then iteratively build compaction candidates by starting with the smallest run and incrementally adding one more run at a time, continuing until all runs are included. Each intermediate compaction is added to the candidate set. A similar strategy is applied for Pattern 2, where the runs in $(i+1)$-th level are also sorted and incrementally included. For Pattern 3, although a similar incremental approach can be applied, the resulting compaction candidate set can still grow to an enormous size when the total number of levels is large. Therefore, in practice, we typically limit the number of levels to fewer than 8[^1]. By doing this pruning, Arce is able to rapidly find the valid compaction set in 30us in our experiment.

**Figure 3:** Illustration of how the tree state evolves when the MemTable is flushed or a compaction finishes.

**Figure 4:** Example of timestamp ranges of different levels.

**Figure 5:** An example of an ElasticLSM within a count window.

[^1]: The default number of levels in RocksDB is 7.
sections.translationMarkdown:
ElasticLSM 维护跨多个层的一组 sorted run，每个 run 的大小和数量都可能不同。在不使用层容量或最大 run 数量等固定结构参数的情况下，系统必须显式决定何时以及如何执行两种核心管理动作：compaction 和 write stall。ElasticLSM 中的 write stall 很直接：仅当 sorted run 总数超过可调阈值 $c$ 时，才以停顿速率 $k$ 对更新进行限速。这种灵活性使系统能够更好地平衡读写吞吐量。两个参数都可以独立调节，详见第 §3.4 节。下面详细介绍更复杂的动作——compaction。

**广泛的 Compaction 选项。** 移除层容量、sorted run 数量和 run 大小等结构约束后，任意一层都可以包含任意数量、大小各异的 sorted run。然而，这种灵活性并不意味着可以随意合并任意 run 子集。LSM-tree 的核心要求是维持跨层的时间戳顺序：较浅层中的时间戳必须大于较深层中的时间戳，而同一层内的 sorted run 可以具有重叠的时间戳（见第 §2 节）。如图 4 所示，同一层中各 run 的时间戳范围可以重叠，而底层相较于顶层必须具有互不相交且更小的时间戳。此外，遵循 LSM-tree 的传统，我们将 compaction 限制为向下进行，以免使同一层内 run 的时间戳顺序复杂化。

基于这些规则，我们识别出三种能够产生有效 compaction 候选集的模式：

- **模式 1（层内）：** compact 第 $i$ 层任意两个或更多 sorted run，并将结果放入第 $i$ 层。
- **模式 2（相邻层）：** compact 第 $i$ 层的全部 sorted run，以及第 $(i+1)$ 层的零个或多个 sorted run，并将结果放入第 $(i+1)$ 层。
- **模式 3（多层）：** compact 从第 $i$ 层到第 $j$ 层（$j>i+1$）的全部 sorted run，以及第 $(j+1)$ 层的零个或多个 sorted run，并将结果放入第 $(j+1)$ 层。

总体而言，模式 1 支持层内 compaction，模式 2 执行传统的相邻层 compaction，模式 3 支持多层 compaction。尽管这些模式带来了广泛的 compaction 候选项，但由此形成的候选集可能极其庞大，对其进行穷举处理的计算成本很高。为解决这一问题，我们采用启发式剪枝。我们观察到，对于被 compact 数据量相近的情况，减少更多 sorted run 通常会带来更好的查找性能。因此，对于模式 1，我们并不枚举一层内所有可能的 run 组合，而是先按大小升序排列各 run。随后，从最小的 run 开始迭代构建 compaction 候选项，每次逐步再加入一个 run，直到纳入所有 run 为止。每个中间 compaction 都被加入候选集。模式 2 采用类似策略，其中第 $(i+1)$ 层的 run 也会排序并逐步加入。对于模式 3，虽然可以采用类似的增量方法，但当总层数较大时，所得 compaction 候选集仍可能增长到极其庞大的规模。因此，在实践中，我们通常将层数限制在 8 层以下[^1]。通过这种剪枝，实验中 Arce 能够在 30us 内快速找到有效 compaction 集。

**图 3：** MemTable flush 或 compaction 完成时 tree state 如何演化的示意图。

**图 4：** 不同层的时间戳范围示例。

**图 5：** count window 内 ElasticLSM 的示例。

[^1]: RocksDB 的默认层数为 7。

sections.title: 3.2 System Cost Modeling
sections.originalMarkdown:
Since ElasticLSM greatly expands the action space, it is crucial to understand how different compaction strategies and write stall parameters influence overall performance before making decisions. In traditional LSM-trees, operational costs are straightforward to predict because compactions and stalls follow fixed patterns. In contrast, our flexible design makes cost estimation more challenging, as the tree state (i.e., the sorted runs in the tree) can evolve by more flexible and unpredictable actions. To address this, we introduce a *Windowed-State Cost Modeling* method, which partitions the long running operation sequence into multiple state-stable windows, where tree state is generally unchanged. We then estimate the three operational costs within each window and define the rules for state transitions between consecutive windows.

**Count Window: Maintaining a Stable Tree State.** As discussed in Section §2, both range and point lookup costs depend on the number of sorted runs. In ElasticLSM, removing structural constraints enables more flexible compactions, but also makes the number of runs highly unpredictable. We observe that the most frequent change in run count occurs when a full MemTable is flushed to the first level (L0), whereas compactions, although they also modify the run count, generally take longer to complete. Based on this observation, we partition foreground operations into consecutive windows, each containing $u=F/E$ updates, where $F$ is the MemTable size and $E$ is the entry size. We term these *count windows* (or simply, windows), inspired by stream processing techniques [34]. The number of range lookup and point lookup within a window are denoted as $r$ and $p$ respectively. And naturally, we can describe the workload pattern by $(r,u,p)$ tuple. Within a window, we maintain a relatively stable tree state $S$, defined as the set of sorted runs and their sizes. As illustrated in Figure 5, the example shows a stable tree state within a window containing six sorted runs of sizes $a_1$ to $a_6$ across three levels. This stable state allows us to estimate the cost of the three primary operations within the window as follows.

**Operational Cost in a Window.** For a point lookup, the LSM-tree may scan up to all $s=|S|$ runs to locate the target key. Each run is equipped with a Bloom filter with false positive rate $\alpha$, so I/O to access a data block is required only when the filter returns a positive result. In the worst case, exactly one run yields a true positive, while the others incur I/O only on false positives with probability $\alpha$. The resulting cost is given in Equation 1, where $I_r$ denotes the I/O time to access a data block.

For a range lookup, the system first locates the start position and retrieves the corresponding block from each run, incurring a cost of $s\cdot I_r$. It then sequentially scans $l$ entries (range length) from each run, with I/O cost $lE/B\cdot I_r$, where $E$ is the entry size and $B$ the data block size. Since this scanning phase depends only on $l$ and not on the LSM-tree state, we omit it from subsequent optimization (see Equation 2).

For updates, prior methods tie write stalls to compaction, with stall time proportional to compacted bytes as dictated by structural constraints. In contrast, ElasticLSM decouples compaction from stalling: updates are slowed by a tunable rate $k$ only when $s$ exceeds an independent threshold $c$. The update cost is thus the flush I/O cost plus the stall penalty $k\cdot\mathbb{I}(s>c)$, where $\mathbb{I}$ returns 1 if $s>c$ and 0 otherwise, and $I_w$ is the I/O time to write a block (Equation 3).

$$\text{Point Lookup Cost}\quad P(s)=(\alpha\cdot s+1)\cdot I_r \tag{1}$$

$$\text{Range Lookup Cost}\quad R(s)=s\cdot I_r \tag{2}$$

$$\text{Update Cost}\quad U(s)=(F/B)\cdot I_w+k\cdot\mathbb{I}(s>c) \tag{3}$$

**Evolving Tree State Between Windows.** Once the cost within a single window is known, estimating the cost of the $i$-th window requires understanding how the run count in the tree state changes from $s_{i-1}$ to $s_i$. Such changes occur through two background actions: MemTable flushes and compactions. As shown in Figure 3(a), flushing a MemTable simply adds a new sorted run of size $a_3$ to the state, yielding $s_i=s_{i-1}+1$. In contrast, compaction alters the tree state more intricately, since its completion time is uncertain and typically not aligned with window boundaries. To address this, we note that a compaction in the background thread completes when the total I/O time of foreground operations equals (or exceeds) the compaction’s I/O time when having sufficient I/O bandwidth. Specifically, if a compaction of size $X$ bytes starts in the $i$-th window, it will finish in the $(i+t)$-th window, where the cumulative I/O cost of foreground operations over $t$ windows matches the compaction’s I/O time. The foreground I/O time in $t$ windows without other concurrent compactions is given in Equation 4. To preserve a stable tree state within each window, we consider the compaction to take effect in the next window after completion. The value of $t$ is computed using Equation 5. Experimental results (Figures 11(c) and (d)) show that this rounding has minimal impact on the accuracy of theoretical cost model.

$$f(s,t)=\sum_{i=0}^{t-1} r\cdot R(s+i)+u\cdot U(s+i)+p\cdot P(s+i) \tag{4}$$

$$t=\min\left\{t\in\mathbb{Z}^{+}\mid f(s,t)\geq\frac{X}{B}(I_r+I_w)\right\} \tag{5}$$

*Example 3.1.* As shown in Figure 3(b), for a compaction of size $X$ that removes $y$ sorted runs, if the estimated completion time is after 2 windows, its effect will be applied in the third window by removing the compacted runs (e.g., $a_1$ and $a_2$) and installing the result (e.g., $a_5$).

The number of sorted runs of windows evolves by:

$$s_{i+1}=\begin{cases}s_i+1, & \text{No compaction completes at }i+1\text{ window}\\s_i+1-y, & \text{Compaction reducing }y\text{ runs completes}\end{cases} \tag{6}$$
sections.translationMarkdown:
由于 ElasticLSM 极大扩展了动作空间，因此在做出决策之前，理解不同 compaction 策略和 write stall 参数如何影响整体性能至关重要。在传统 LSM-tree 中，由于 compaction 和 stall 遵循固定模式，操作成本很容易预测。相比之下，我们的灵活设计使成本估计更具挑战性，因为 tree state（即树中的 sorted run）可以通过更加灵活且不可预测的动作演化。为解决这一问题，我们提出一种*窗口化状态成本建模（Windowed-State Cost Modeling）*方法，将长期运行的操作序列划分为多个状态稳定窗口，在每个窗口中 tree state 通常保持不变。随后，我们估计每个窗口内三种操作的成本，并定义相邻窗口之间的状态转换规则。

**计数窗口：维持稳定的 Tree State。** 如第 §2 节所述，范围查找和点查找的成本都取决于 sorted run 的数量。在 ElasticLSM 中，移除结构约束使 compaction 更加灵活，但也使 run 数量变得高度不可预测。我们观察到，run 数量最频繁的变化发生在已满的 MemTable 被 flush 到第一层（L0）时；而 compaction 虽然也会改变 run 数量，但通常需要更长时间才能完成。基于这一观察，我们将前台操作划分为连续的窗口，每个窗口包含 $u=F/E$ 次更新，其中 $F$ 为 MemTable 大小，$E$ 为 entry 大小。受流处理技术 [34] 启发，我们将其称为*计数窗口（count window）*，简称窗口。一个窗口内的范围查找和点查找数量分别记为 $r$ 和 $p$。自然地，可以使用三元组 $(r,u,p)$ 描述工作负载模式。在一个窗口内，我们维持相对稳定的 tree state $S$，其定义为 sorted run 及其大小所构成的集合。如图 5 所示，该示例展示了一个窗口内的稳定 tree state：三个层中包含大小从 $a_1$ 到 $a_6$ 的六个 sorted run。借助这一稳定状态，可以按如下方式估计窗口内三种主要操作的成本。

**窗口内的操作成本。** 对于点查找，LSM-tree 为定位目标 key，最多可能扫描全部 $s=|S|$ 个 run。每个 run 都配有 false positive rate 为 $\alpha$ 的 Bloom filter，因此只有 filter 返回 positive 结果时，才需要执行访问 data block 的 I/O。在最坏情况下，恰好一个 run 产生 true positive，而其他 run 仅以概率 $\alpha$ 因 false positive 产生 I/O。所得成本如公式 1 所示，其中 $I_r$ 表示访问一个 data block 的 I/O 时间。

对于范围查找，系统首先定位起始位置并从每个 run 中读取相应的 block，产生 $s\cdot I_r$ 的成本。随后，系统从每个 run 中顺序扫描 $l$ 个 entry（范围长度），I/O 成本为 $lE/B\cdot I_r$，其中 $E$ 是 entry 大小，$B$ 是 data block 大小。由于该扫描阶段仅依赖 $l$，而不依赖 LSM-tree 状态，因此在后续优化中将其省略（见公式 2）。

对于更新，先前方法将 write stall 与 compaction 绑定在一起；在结构约束的支配下，stall 时间与 compact 的字节数成正比。相比之下，ElasticLSM 将 compaction 与 stall 解耦：仅当 $s$ 超过独立阈值 $c$ 时，才以可调速率 $k$ 降低更新速度。因此，更新成本等于 flush I/O 成本加上 stall 惩罚 $k\cdot\mathbb{I}(s>c)$；当 $s>c$ 时，$\mathbb{I}$ 返回 1，否则返回 0；$I_w$ 为写入一个 block 的 I/O 时间（公式 3）。

$$\text{点查找成本}\quad P(s)=(\alpha\cdot s+1)\cdot I_r \tag{1}$$

$$\text{范围查找成本}\quad R(s)=s\cdot I_r \tag{2}$$

$$\text{更新成本}\quad U(s)=(F/B)\cdot I_w+k\cdot\mathbb{I}(s>c) \tag{3}$$

**窗口之间 Tree State 的演化。** 在已知单个窗口内成本后，要估计第 $i$ 个窗口的成本，就需要理解 tree state 中的 run 数量如何从 $s_{i-1}$ 变化到 $s_i$。这种变化由两种后台动作引起：MemTable flush 和 compaction。如图 3(a) 所示，flush 一个 MemTable 只会向状态中添加一个大小为 $a_3$ 的新 sorted run，从而得到 $s_i=s_{i-1}+1$。相比之下，compaction 对 tree state 的改变更复杂，因为其完成时间不确定，并且通常不与窗口边界对齐。为处理这一问题，我们注意到，在 I/O 带宽充足的情况下，当前台操作的总 I/O 时间等于（或超过）compaction 的 I/O 时间时，后台线程中的 compaction 即告完成。具体而言，如果一个大小为 $X$ 字节的 compaction 在第 $i$ 个窗口启动，那么它将在第 $(i+t)$ 个窗口完成，其中 $t$ 个窗口内前台操作的累计 I/O 成本与 compaction 的 I/O 时间相匹配。在不存在其他并发 compaction 时，$t$ 个窗口内的前台 I/O 时间由公式 4 给出。为了使每个窗口内的 tree state 保持稳定，我们认为 compaction 在完成后的下一个窗口才生效。$t$ 的值使用公式 5 计算。实验结果（图 11(c) 和图 11(d)）表明，这种取整处理对理论成本模型准确性的影响很小。

$$f(s,t)=\sum_{i=0}^{t-1} r\cdot R(s+i)+u\cdot U(s+i)+p\cdot P(s+i) \tag{4}$$

$$t=\min\left\{t\in\mathbb{Z}^{+}\mid f(s,t)\geq\frac{X}{B}(I_r+I_w)\right\} \tag{5}$$

*示例 3.1。* 如图 3(b) 所示，对于一个大小为 $X$、会移除 $y$ 个 sorted run 的 compaction，如果其预计在经过 2 个窗口后完成，那么它将在第三个窗口中生效：移除被 compact 的 run（例如 $a_1$ 和 $a_2$），并安装结果（例如 $a_5$）。

各窗口中的 sorted run 数量按如下方式演化：

$$s_{i+1}=\begin{cases}s_i+1, & \text{在第 }i+1\text{ 个窗口没有 compaction 完成}\\s_i+1-y, & \text{减少 }y\text{ 个 run 的 compaction 完成}\end{cases} \tag{6}$$

sections.title: 3.3 Arce: Decide the Intermediate Compaction
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Objective Function.** Based on the cost model and state-evolution rules defined above, we can express the average cost for a given workload $(r,u,p)$ with stall parameters $c$ and $k$, after performing $m$ compactions, as:

$$C=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}f(s_i,t_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}t_i(r+u+p)} \tag{7}$$

subject to the update rule:

$$s_{i+1}=s_i+t_i-y_i \tag{8}$$

Here, each compaction has $X_i$ bytes, reduces $y_i$ sorted runs, spans over $t_i$ count windows, and starts with $s_i$ sorted runs. The function $f(s_i,t_i)$ represents the cumulative cost over window $t_i$, as defined earlier. This problem is fundamentally a search problem to find out $m$ compaction to minimize the average cost. Evaluating only short-term compaction candidates (i.e., small $m$) is computationally efficient, but tends to favor smaller, quickly completed compactions and larger stall thresholds $c$, which yield short-term benefits by reducing run count more rapidly. However, such strategies may overlook larger compactions that, although expensive upfront, offer substantial long-term benefits. For example, merging two 40GiB runs may yield sustained lookup improvements for the next 40GiB of inserted data. Exploring deeper compaction sequences to capture these long-term gains introduces significant computational overhead and can be proven to be NP-hard. Formal proof is provided in our technical report [1].

*Lemma 3.2.* Deciding $m$ compactions to minimize Equation 7 is NP-hard.

**Figure 6:** The left panel defines dominating compactions, while the right panel illustrates their benefits.

Fortunately, it is unnecessary to determine the full sequence of $m$ compactions in advance. Instead, we only need to identify the first compaction to execute at each decision point. This raises a key question: Can we design a principled method to quantify both the short-term penalty and long-term benefit of an intermediate compaction candidate?

**Short-Term Effect.** The immediate drawback—or penalty—of executing a compaction is that it occupies a background compaction worker, potentially causing SSTs to accumulate at L0. This accumulation can degrade read performance and even trigger a write stall. We model the short-term cost as:

$$E_s(s,t)=\underbrace{I_r\cdot t\cdot(r+\alpha\cdot p)}_{\text{Read slowdown}}+\underbrace{uk\cdot\max(0,s+t-c)}_{\text{Write stall penalty}} \tag{9}$$

Here, $t$ denotes the estimated duration of compaction with sorted runs $s$ in the system.

**Long-Term Effect.** Compaction reduces the number of sorted runs, which benefits all future reads within the current decision window. We define the long-term benefit of a compaction that reduces $y$ sorted runs as:

$$E_l(y)=(r+\alpha\cdot p)\cdot I_r\cdot y \tag{10}$$

**Effectiveness Score.** By integrating both effects, we define the overall effectiveness of a compaction spanning $t$ windows and reducing $y$ runs as:

$$E(s,t,y)=M\cdot E_l(y)-E_s(s,t) \tag{11}$$

The parameter $M$ scales the long-term benefit and is determined by the current tree state, workload characteristics, and write stall threshold. Section 3.4 provides guidance on selecting the appropriate $(M,c,k)$ under different scenarios. Given a fixed $(M,c,k)$, Arce can select the compaction with the highest effective score among many compaction candidates.

**Optimality Analysis.** The effectiveness score not only significantly improves the efficiency of compaction selection but also reveals an important structural property among compaction candidates—domination. Formally, we say that compaction $A$ dominates compaction $B$ (denoted as $B\prec A$) if and only if $A$ reduces more sorted runs as $B$ while requiring less compaction time. Using the score-based evaluation defined in Equation 11, Arce ensures that only non-dominated candidates are selected under any given parameter configuration $(M,c,k)$. We refer to these as *dominating compactions*, which collectively form the left frontier in a two-dimensional space, where the x-axis represents elapsed time $t$ and the y-axis represents the number of reduced sorted runs $y$, as illustrated in Figure 6.

*Lemma 3.3.* If $A\prec B$, the effectiveness score of $A$ is less than $B$.

*Proof.* The long-term effect of $B$ will increase by $(r+\alpha\cdot p)\cdot I_r\cdot(y_2-y_1)$, while the short-term effect of $B$ will decrease by at least $I_r\cdot(t_1-t_2)\cdot(r+\alpha\cdot p)+uk\cdot\max(0,t_1-t_2+s-c)$. Therefore, the effectiveness score of $B$ is larger than $A$. □

In our extended version [1], we show that every dominating compaction can be selected by some $(M,c,k)$, and at least part of the dominating compaction must occur in the optimal sequence.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**目标函数。** 根据上述成本模型和状态演化规则，对于给定工作负载 $(r,u,p)$ 以及 stall 参数 $c$ 和 $k$，执行 $m$ 次 compaction 后的平均成本可表示为：

$$C=\frac{\sum_{i=1}^{m}f(s_i,t_i)}{\sum_{i=1}^{m}t_i(r+u+p)} \tag{7}$$

并满足如下更新规则：

$$s_{i+1}=s_i+t_i-y_i \tag{8}$$

这里，每次 compaction 的数据量为 $X_i$ 字节，会减少 $y_i$ 个 sorted run，跨越 $t_i$ 个 count window，并从系统中存在 $s_i$ 个 sorted run 的状态开始。函数 $f(s_i,t_i)$ 表示 $t_i$ 个窗口内的累计成本，其定义见前文。该问题本质上是一个搜索问题，即寻找 $m$ 次 compaction 以最小化平均成本。仅评估短期 compaction 候选项（即较小的 $m$）计算效率较高，但往往倾向于选择规模较小、能够快速完成的 compaction，以及更大的 stall 阈值 $c$；这些选择通过更快减少 run 数量来获得短期收益。然而，这类策略可能忽略规模更大的 compaction：尽管其前期开销较高，却能提供显著的长期收益。例如，合并两个 40GiB 的 run，可能会持续改善接下来插入的 40GiB 数据的查找性能。为捕获这些长期收益而探索更深的 compaction 序列会引入显著计算开销，并且可以证明该问题是 NP-hard。形式化证明见我们的技术报告 [1]。

*引理 3.2。* 决定使公式 7 最小化的 $m$ 次 compaction 是 NP-hard 问题。

**图 6：** 左图定义了支配性 compaction，右图说明了它们的收益。

幸运的是，无需预先决定由 $m$ 次 compaction 构成的完整序列。相反，我们只需在每个决策点识别首个要执行的 compaction。这引出了一个关键问题：能否设计一种有原则的方法，同时量化一个中间 compaction 候选项的短期惩罚和长期收益？

**短期效应。** 执行 compaction 的直接缺点（或惩罚）是它会占用一个后台 compaction worker，可能导致 SST 在 L0 累积。这种累积会降低读性能，甚至触发 write stall。我们将短期成本建模为：

$$E_s(s,t)=\underbrace{I_r\cdot t\cdot(r+\alpha\cdot p)}_{\text{读性能下降}}+\underbrace{uk\cdot\max(0,s+t-c)}_{\text{Write stall 惩罚}} \tag{9}$$

这里，$t$ 表示系统中存在 $s$ 个 sorted run 时，compaction 的预计持续时间。

**长期效应。** Compaction 会减少 sorted run 的数量，从而使当前决策窗口内未来的所有读取受益。我们将减少 $y$ 个 sorted run 的 compaction 所带来的长期收益定义为：

$$E_l(y)=(r+\alpha\cdot p)\cdot I_r\cdot y \tag{10}$$

**有效性得分。** 综合两种效应，我们将一个跨越 $t$ 个窗口并减少 $y$ 个 run 的 compaction 的总体有效性定义为：

$$E(s,t,y)=M\cdot E_l(y)-E_s(s,t) \tag{11}$$

参数 $M$ 对长期收益进行缩放，它由当前 tree state、工作负载特征和 write stall 阈值共同决定。第 3.4 节介绍了如何在不同场景下选择合适的 $(M,c,k)$。在固定 $(M,c,k)$ 的情况下，Arce 可以从众多 compaction 候选项中选出有效性得分最高的 compaction。

**最优性分析。** 有效性得分不仅显著提高了 compaction 选择的效率，还揭示了 compaction 候选项之间一个重要的结构性质——支配关系。形式化地，当且仅当 compaction $A$ 比 compaction $B$ 减少更多 sorted run，同时需要更少的 compaction 时间时，我们称 compaction $A$ 支配 compaction $B$（记作 $B\prec A$）。利用公式 11 定义的基于得分的评估，Arce 确保在任意给定参数配置 $(M,c,k)$ 下，只选择非支配候选项。我们将这些候选项称为*支配性 compaction*。如图 6 所示，它们共同构成一个二维空间中的左侧前沿，其中 x 轴表示经过的时间 $t$，y 轴表示减少的 sorted run 数量 $y$。

*引理 3.3。* 如果 $A\prec B$，则 $A$ 的有效性得分低于 $B$。

*证明。* $B$ 的长期效应将增加 $(r+\alpha\cdot p)\cdot I_r\cdot(y_2-y_1)$，而 $B$ 的短期效应将至少减少 $I_r\cdot(t_1-t_2)\cdot(r+\alpha\cdot p)+uk\cdot\max(0,t_1-t_2+s-c)$。因此，$B$ 的有效性得分高于 $A$。□

在扩展版本 [1] 中，我们证明了每个支配性 compaction 都可以由某个 $(M,c,k)$ 选中，并且支配性 compaction 的至少一部分必然出现在最优序列中。

sections.title: 3.4 Parameter Selection
4 ARCEKV: WORKLOAD-DRIVEN KV STORE
sections.titleTranslation: 4 ARCEKV：工作负载驱动的键值存储
sections.originalMarkdown:
As shown in Figure 7, ArceKV, built upon Arce introduced earlier, incorporates four key components: Workload Statistics, ElasticLSM, Background Simulation, and Arce’s score-based compaction selection. The Workload Statistics module monitors operations and reports windowed counts $(r,u,p)$ every one million operations. The Background Simulation module implements Algorithm 1, outputting $c$ and $k$ to RocksDB’s write controller to manage write stalls, and forwarding $M$, $c$, and $k$ to Arce’s score-based selection module to determine optimal compactions according to Equation 11. ElasticLSM manages SSTables organized into sorted runs and levels, which Arce dynamically compacts based on these decisions. All components are implemented within RocksDB. The following section details the implementation.

**Figure 7: Overview of the architecture of ArceKV**

**Parallel Simulation.** Unlike tree traversal, the score-based simulation is inherently parallelizable, as each $(M,c,k)$ tuple can be evaluated independently. Distributing the computation across multiple threads can significantly accelerate the algorithm. By default, ArceKV uses 16 threads to run these simulations, which complete well within a single window. Additionally, the effectiveness scores are computed through linear transformations of compaction size $X$, reduced runs $y$, and elapsed windows $t$. This structure allows us to vectorize the score computation across all compaction candidates using the Eigen library [39]. Eigen applies SIMD (Single Instruction, Multiple Data) optimizations under the hood, further improving simulation efficiency.

**Parameter Pruning.** To reduce computational overhead, we adopt coarse-grained parameter tuning. For $M$, we use a step size of 5, as nearby values yield similar compaction choices; for $c$, we use a step size of 2, since closely spaced $(M,c)$ pairs produce comparable results. We also set upper bounds: $M$ is capped at the smallest value selecting the compaction with the largest reduction in sorted runs (upper-right candidate in Figure 6(a)), and $c$ is limited to less than $4\times$ the current run count, as exceeding this should already trigger re-selection based on the change threshold in the previous section. For the write stall penalty $k$, performance changes significantly only when it is doubled or halved. Thus, we initialize $k=6$ (RocksDB’s default stall rate) and test two additional values by successive doubling, as finer granularity provides diminishing returns. Finally, we cap the simulation iterations for each parameter tuple at MaxIterTime (typically 400) to ensure completion before the tree state drifts, while keeping the duration long enough to capture long-term benefits. Under a balanced workload ($r=u=p$), a window lasts over 200 ms and simulation completes within 150ms, keeping the system responsive during parameter selection.

**Multi-threading Extension.** The cost model in Section §3.2 assumes a single foreground thread and one background compaction worker, whereas real systems typically execute multiple foreground threads concurrently. Although the per-operation I/O cost remains unchanged, parallel execution reduces the number of elapsed compaction windows. Following Cosine [14], we model this effect using Amdahl’s Law [5]. With $\eta$ cores and parallelizable fraction $\phi$, the speedup is $g=\frac{1}{1-\phi(1-1/\eta)}$, yielding an adjusted compaction duration $t'=t/g$. Based on profiling, we set $\phi=0.5$.

To support multiple background workers, ArceKV tracks available compaction threads. When multiple workers are idle, newly flushed SSTables are assumed to compact immediately and no penalty is applied; otherwise, a delay penalty is introduced. The system also monitors thread, memory, and I/O utilization, applying a large penalty when resources become saturated.
sections.translationMarkdown:
如图 7 所示，建立在前文所述 Arce 之上的 ArceKV 包含四个关键组件：Workload Statistics、ElasticLSM、Background Simulation，以及 Arce 基于评分的 compaction 选择模块。Workload Statistics 模块监控操作，并且每一百万次操作报告一次窗口内计数 $(r,u,p)$。Background Simulation 模块实现算法 1，将 $c$ 和 $k$ 输出给 RocksDB 的写控制器以管理写停顿，并将 $M$、$c$ 和 $k$ 转发给 Arce 基于评分的选择模块，由其依据公式 11 确定最优 compaction。ElasticLSM 管理组织为 sorted run 和 level 的 SSTable，Arce 根据上述决策对其进行动态 compaction。所有组件均在 RocksDB 内实现。下面详细介绍其实现。

**图 7：ArceKV 的体系结构概览**

**并行模拟。** 与树遍历不同，基于评分的模拟天然可以并行化，因为每个 $(M,c,k)$ 元组都能独立评估。将计算分配给多个线程可以显著加速该算法。默认情况下，ArceKV 使用 16 个线程运行这些模拟，并且能在单个窗口内完成。此外，有效性评分由 compaction 大小 $X$、减少的 run 数量 $y$ 和经过的窗口数 $t$ 进行线性变换得到。借助这一结构，我们可以使用 Eigen 库 [39] 对所有 compaction 候选项的评分计算进行向量化。Eigen 在底层应用 SIMD（单指令多数据）优化，从而进一步提高模拟效率。

**参数剪枝。** 为降低计算开销，我们采用粗粒度参数调优。对于 $M$，步长设为 5，因为相邻取值会产生相似的 compaction 选择；对于 $c$，步长设为 2，因为相近的 $(M,c)$ 对会产生可比的结果。我们还设置了上界：$M$ 的上限是能够选中“减少 sorted run 数量最多的 compaction”（图 6(a) 右上角候选项）的最小值；$c$ 则被限制为小于当前 run 数量的 $4\times$，因为超过该值时，依据上一节的变化阈值本应已经触发重新选择。对于写停顿惩罚 $k$，只有当其翻倍或减半时性能才会显著变化。因此，我们将 $k$ 初始化为 6（RocksDB 的默认停顿速率），并通过连续翻倍再测试两个值，因为更细的粒度只能带来递减的收益。最后，我们将每个参数元组的模拟迭代次数限制为 MaxIterTime（通常为 400），以保证在树状态发生漂移之前完成，同时又让模拟持续足够长的时间以捕捉长期收益。在均衡工作负载（$r=u=p$）下，一个窗口持续超过 200 ms，模拟在 150 ms 内完成，因此系统在参数选择期间仍能及时响应。

**多线程扩展。** 第 §3.2 节的代价模型假定只有一个前台线程和一个后台 compaction worker，而真实系统通常会并发执行多个前台线程。尽管单次操作的 I/O 代价不变，并行执行会减少 compaction 所经历的窗口数量。依照 Cosine [14]，我们使用 Amdahl 定律 [5] 建模这一影响。当核心数为 $\eta$、可并行比例为 $\phi$ 时，加速比为 $g=\frac{1}{1-\phi(1-1/\eta)}$，由此得到调整后的 compaction 持续时间 $t'=t/g$。根据性能剖析结果，我们将 $\phi$ 设为 0.5。

为了支持多个后台 worker，ArceKV 会跟踪可用的 compaction 线程。当多个 worker 空闲时，系统假定新 flush 的 SSTable 会立即执行 compaction，不施加惩罚；否则便引入延迟惩罚。系统还监控线程、内存和 I/O 利用率，并在资源饱和时施加较大的惩罚。

sections.title: 5 EVALUATION
sections.originalMarkdown:
This section presents the experimental evaluation of ArceKV. All experiments are conducted on a machine equipped with an Intel Core i9-13900K CPU (5.40GHz), 128GB of RAM, and a 1TB NVMe SSD, running 64-bit Ubuntu 22.04 with an ext4 file system. To simulate realistic deployment scenarios, where not all system memory is allocated to RocksDB (e.g., TiKV recommends allocating 70% [69]), We follow Disco [106] in using the cgroup command to cap total memory usage at 75GiB. In terms of baselines, we include both widely adopted compaction strategies, such as Leveling [37], 1-Leveling [33], and Tiering [53], and recently published academic state-of-the-art LSM-tree systems with available artifacts, including LazyLeveling [26], Moose [58], Ruskey [64], and CAMAL [98]. These systems are all capable of handling point lookups, range queries, and updates issued in random order and speed using a unified handler thread. To ensure fair comparison and practical generalizability, we adopt RocksDB’s default configuration, using one background compaction thread and one flush thread. Additionally, to broaden the evaluation of effectiveness, we include industrial-grade systems such as Pebble [52], WiredTiger [19], and Cassandra [53] as baselines.

**Baselines.** The following systems and compaction strategies are used as baselines:

- **Leveling (abbr. Lvl):** Maintains at most one sorted run per level and increases level capacity using a fixed size ratio $T$. This policy is optimal for read-intensive workloads.
- **Tiering (abbr. Tier):** Allows up to $T$ sorted runs per level, also growing capacity by size ratio $T$. It is designed to favor write-intensive workloads by minimizing compaction overhead.
- **1-Leveling (abbr. 1-L):** The default compaction style in RocksDB. Unlike traditional Leveling, it allows up to 20 sorted runs at the first level, making it particularly effective for read-heavy workloads and also adaptive to workloads with a portion of writes.
- **LazyLeveling (abbr. LL):** Structurally similar to Tiering but maintains only one sorted run at the largest level. This hybrid design improves performance for mixed read-write workloads.
- **Moose (abbr. MSE):** Leverages a dynamic programming algorithm to configure an LSM-tree structure that achieves an optimal balance among point lookups, range queries, and updates based on the given workload.
- **Ruskey (abbr. RKY):** Uses a reinforcement learning (RL) model to guide structural transitions, reducing the overhead of adapting to new workloads. Ruskey fixes the size ratio at $T=10$, while the number of sorted runs at each level is determined by the RL policy.
- **CAMAL (abbr. CAM):** Employs active learning to optimize structural parameters, such as size ratio and the number of sorted runs per level as well as the allocation of memory to Bloom filter, cache, and Memtable.

**Implementation of Baselines.** To ensure a fair comparison, all compaction policies except Ruskey[^2] and CAMAL[^3] are implemented on top of the Moose codebase[^4], a framework based on RocksDB that supports configurable numbers of sorted runs and level capacities. Leveling, Tiering, and LazyLeveling are implemented using this framework, each configured with a size ratio of $T=10$. For Moose, the size ratios and the number of sorted runs per level are determined by its dynamic programming algorithm. In our evaluation, we set these values to 40, 40, 41 for size ratios and 6, 6, 6 for sorted runs, ensuring they accommodate the total number of entries to be ingested. ArceKV (abbr. ARC) implements on top of RocksDB and sets the total level to 4, the I/O cost $I_w=15\,\mu s$ and $I_r=12\,\mu s$ based on the system profiling result. We use a Bloom filter with 10 bits-per-key for all baselines, following RocksDB’s default implementation. Keys are fixed at 24 bytes, and values at 1,000 bytes and the write buffer is 2MB which is consistent with configurations commonly used in prior studies [26, 58, 64].

**Dynamic Workload Generation.** To construct realistic dynamic workloads, we primarily follow the approach used in Ruskey, which involves combining multiple sub-workloads with varying read-write ratios. To further enhance flexibility and realism, we distinguish between point lookups and range lookups when composing these workloads. We adopt several typical workload configurations evaluated in Endure [43] and Moose [58], with their compositions summarized in Table 2. We define two primary compound workloads:

**Table 2: Operation Ratios Composition**

| Operation | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I | J |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| range(%) | 98 | 1 | 1 | 49 | 2 | 49 | 40 | 40 | 20 | 33 |
| update(%) | 1 | 98 | 1 | 2 | 49 | 49 | 40 | 20 | 40 | 33 |
| point(%) | 1 | 1 | 98 | 49 | 49 | 2 | 20 | 40 | 40 | 33 |

- **Workload I:** This compound workload consists of A, B, D, J, C, and E, with each sub-workload containing 40,960,000 operations, totaling 245,760,000 operations. It inserts approximately 74GiB of new data.
- **Workload II:** This workload comprises J, E, B, F, D, and C, with 40,960,000 operations per sub-workload, totaling 245,760,000 operations and inserting 94GiB new data.

The workload ratios in Workload I shift more abruptly—from a high write ratio to a low one—while Workload II exhibits a more gradual transition, with the write ratio slowly decreasing over time. For multi-threaded evaluation and comparison with industrial KV stores, we use Workload III, composed of G, H, and I workloads, each with 20,480,000 operations. This setup avoids extreme read- or write-heavy cases that are less reflective of production scenarios. Before running any test workload, we preload the system by sequentially writing 40GiB of data (about 40 million entries) to ensure a stable compaction state, enabling fair evaluation of write-friendly strategies like Tiering and LazyLeveling.

[^2]: The implementation of Ruskey was obtained from the original authors. It is built on RocksDB.
[^3]: https://github.com/NTU-Siqiang-Group/CAMAL
[^4]: https://github.com/NTU-Siqiang-Group/MooseLSM
sections.translationMarkdown:
本节给出对 ArceKV 的实验评估。所有实验均在一台配备 Intel Core i9-13900K CPU（5.40GHz）、128GB RAM 和 1TB NVMe SSD 的机器上进行，操作系统为 64 位 Ubuntu 22.04，文件系统为 ext4。为了模拟并非全部系统内存都会分配给 RocksDB 的真实部署场景（例如 TiKV 建议分配 70% [69]），我们沿用 Disco [106] 的做法，通过 cgroup 命令将总内存用量限制在 75GiB。基线既包括 Leveling [37]、1-Leveling [33] 和 Tiering [53] 等广泛采用的 compaction 策略，也包括近期发表且提供 artifact 的学术界先进 LSM-tree 系统 LazyLeveling [26]、Moose [58]、Ruskey [64] 和 CAMAL [98]。这些系统都能使用统一的处理线程，以随机的顺序和速率处理 point lookup、range query 和 update。为了保证比较公平且结果具有实际普适性，我们采用 RocksDB 的默认配置，即一个后台 compaction 线程和一个 flush 线程。此外，为拓宽有效性评估范围，我们还加入 Pebble [52]、WiredTiger [19] 和 Cassandra [53] 等工业级系统作为基线。

**基线。** 使用以下系统和 compaction 策略作为基线：

- **Leveling（缩写 Lvl）：** 每层最多维护一个 sorted run，并使用固定大小比率 $T$ 增大 level 容量。该策略最适合读密集型工作负载。
- **Tiering（缩写 Tier）：** 每层最多允许 $T$ 个 sorted run，容量同样按大小比率 $T$ 增长。该策略通过尽量减少 compaction 开销来偏向写密集型工作负载。
- **1-Leveling（缩写 1-L）：** RocksDB 的默认 compaction 风格。不同于传统 Leveling，它允许第一层最多存在 20 个 sorted run，因此对读密集型工作负载尤其有效，也能适应包含一定比例写操作的负载。
- **LazyLeveling（缩写 LL）：** 结构与 Tiering 相似，但在最大层仅维护一个 sorted run。这种混合设计改善了读写混合负载下的性能。
- **Moose（缩写 MSE）：** 使用动态规划算法配置 LSM-tree 结构，根据给定负载在 point lookup、range query 和 update 之间取得最优平衡。
- **Ruskey（缩写 RKY）：** 使用强化学习（RL）模型指导结构转换，降低适应新负载的开销。Ruskey 将大小比率固定为 $T=10$，每层的 sorted run 数量则由 RL 策略决定。
- **CAMAL（缩写 CAM）：** 使用主动学习优化结构参数，包括大小比率、每层 sorted run 数量，以及分配给 Bloom filter、cache 和 Memtable 的内存。

**基线实现。** 为保证公平比较，除 Ruskey[^2] 和 CAMAL[^3] 外，所有 compaction 策略都基于 Moose 代码库[^4]实现。该框架建立在 RocksDB 之上，支持配置 sorted run 数量和 level 容量。Leveling、Tiering 和 LazyLeveling 均使用该框架实现，大小比率都设为 $T=10$。Moose 各层的大小比率和 sorted run 数量由其动态规划算法决定。在实验中，为确保能够容纳将要写入的全部 entry，我们将大小比率设为 40、40、41，将 sorted run 数量设为 6、6、6。ArceKV（缩写 ARC）基于 RocksDB 实现，将总 level 数设为 4，并根据系统性能剖析结果将 I/O 代价设为 $I_w=15\,\mu s$、$I_r=12\,\mu s$。依照 RocksDB 默认实现，所有基线均使用 10 bits-per-key 的 Bloom filter。key 固定为 24 字节，value 固定为 1,000 字节，write buffer 为 2MB，这与既有研究常用配置一致 [26, 58, 64]。

**动态工作负载生成。** 为构造真实的动态负载，我们主要沿用 Ruskey 的方法，即组合具有不同读写比例的多个子负载。为了进一步提高灵活性和真实性，在组合负载时还区分 point lookup 与 range lookup。我们采用 Endure [43] 和 Moose [58] 中评估过的若干典型负载配置，其组成见表 2。我们定义了两种主要的复合负载：

**表 2：操作比例组成**

| 操作 | A | B | C | D | E | F | G | H | I | J |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| range(%) | 98 | 1 | 1 | 49 | 2 | 49 | 40 | 40 | 20 | 33 |
| update(%) | 1 | 98 | 1 | 2 | 49 | 49 | 40 | 20 | 40 | 33 |
| point(%) | 1 | 1 | 98 | 49 | 49 | 2 | 20 | 40 | 40 | 33 |

- **Workload I：** 该复合负载由 A、B、D、J、C 和 E 组成，每个子负载包含 40,960,000 次操作，总计 245,760,000 次操作，约写入 74GiB 新数据。
- **Workload II：** 该负载由 J、E、B、F、D 和 C 组成，每个子负载包含 40,960,000 次操作，总计 245,760,000 次操作，并写入 94GiB 新数据。

Workload I 的负载比例变化更突然——从高写比例骤变为低写比例；Workload II 的转换则更平缓，写比例随时间逐渐下降。对于多线程评估以及与工业 KV store 的比较，我们使用由 G、H 和 I 组成的 Workload III，每个负载包含 20,480,000 次操作。该设置避开不太能反映生产场景的极端读密集或写密集情况。在运行任何测试负载之前，我们先顺序写入 40GiB 数据（约 4,000 万个 entry）进行预加载，以保证 compaction 状态稳定，从而公平评估 Tiering 和 LazyLeveling 等写友好型策略。

[^2]: Ruskey 的实现从原作者处获得，并基于 RocksDB 构建。
[^3]: https://github.com/NTU-Siqiang-Group/CAMAL
[^4]: https://github.com/NTU-Siqiang-Group/MooseLSM

sections.title: 5.1 System Performance
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Overall, ArceKV demonstrates consistently strong performance under both Workload I and Workload II.** Figure 8 compares seven methods across two long-running workloads, Workload I and Workload II, each composed of multiple subworkloads with diverse read-write ratios, totaling up to 140GiB of data. Across both workloads, ArceKV consistently maintains top-tier performance, ranking first in nearly all subworkloads, except during the transition from workload B to workload D. This specific transition involves a sharp shift from an extremely write-intensive workload (B) to a read-intensive one (D), posing a significant challenge for systems to adapt promptly. Despite this abrupt change, ArceKV still performs competitively, only slightly trailing behind the two read-optimized baselines, 1-Leveling and Leveling. In Workload II, the transition from F to D is less drastic. Here, ArceKV performs similarly with 1-Leveling and Leveling. While these two baselines are optimized for read-heavy workloads, they struggle in write-intensive scenarios due to frequent and aggressive compactions. Conversely, Tiering and LazyLeveling allow more sorted runs per level and adopt lazier compaction strategies, reducing write stall and performing well under write-heavy workloads, but at the cost of degraded read performance due to excessive run accumulation.

Moose does not offer a robust solution for adapting structural configurations across varying workloads. The configurations it generates differ significantly (e.g., size ratios changing from 7,7,7,6,5 to 24,23,24), making direct transitions between them impractical without incurring severe performance degradation. To mitigate this, we compute a unified configuration for Moose based on the total number of inserted entries and the average workload composition. Similarly, since CAMAL is also designed for static workloads, we provide its trained model with the average workload composition to derive the structural parameters. While both CAMAL and Moose perform well overall under this setting, their performance deteriorates significantly in extreme workloads such as B and E, falling well behind the top-performing baselines. In contrast, Ruskey dynamically computes the most suitable configuration and employs an efficient adaptation strategy. While Ruskey performs satisfactorily in scenarios with gradual workload changes or sufficient updates, its adaptation can lag under abrupt shifts or under read-intensive workload. As shown in Figure 9(a) and (b), Ruskey achieves a convergence rate comparable to ArceKV under workload F with sufficient updates, but adapts more slowly during transitions to highly read-intensive workloads such as D.

Furthermore, as shown in Figure 8(c) and (d), ArceKV maintains consistently low P99.9 latency over time, without incurring significant write stall or read overhead compared to other methods. This demonstrates the robustness and stability of ArceKV under varying workload conditions.

**Figure 8: Average latencies and P99.9 latencies for all the methods under Workload I and Workload II.**

**ArceKV orchestrates compactions and stalls more intelligently through the flexible LSM structure ElasticLSM.** Figure 9 presents additional metrics from Workload I to highlight the benefits of ElasticLSM’s structural flexibility. Under read-intensive workloads such as A and D, ElasticLSM is able to reduce the number of sorted runs more quickly than even Leveling by performing multi-level compactions. In contrast, under write-intensive workloads like B, ElasticLSM defers compactions more aggressively than Tiering and LazyLeveling by allowing more sorted runs to accumulate in the system. This enables ArceKV to maintain relatively low write stall time while still performing compactions as eagerly as Leveling when necessary. As a result, ArceKV achieves nearly the same read I/O efficiency as 1-Leveling over time, demonstrating its ability to strike a dynamic balance between compaction aggressiveness and write stall control.

**Figure 9: The table (left) shows the normalized throughput of each sub-workload in Workload I and Workload II; the figures (right) present the change of write stall, total compaction bytes, the change of sorted runs, and the total read I/O when executing Workload I.**

**ArceKV exhibits superior scalability under concurrent workloads than other baselines.** As shown in Figure 10(c), we evaluate six methods (excluding Ruskey and CAMAL) using Workload III under 1, 4, 8, and 16 foreground threads. Ruskey and CAMAL are omitted because they rely on collecting performance metrics from the system and lack synchronization mechanisms for multi-threaded scenarios. Additionally, the Moose framework does not support multiple background workers, so we fix the number of background threads to one and vary only the number of foreground query threads. Overall, as the number of threads increases, all methods show improved throughput, but ArceKV achieves the greatest gains. This is because higher thread counts intensify updates, accelerating sorted run accumulation. Methods like 1-Leveling and Leveling struggle with frequent write stalls due to low stall thresholds, while Tiering and LazyLeveling perform better thanks to higher thresholds. In contrast, ArceKV dynamically adjusts its stall threshold and compaction scheduling (Section §4) to account for multi-threading, effectively balancing read and write performance and delivering the highest scalability.

**ArceKV outperforms other industrial key-value stores.** As shown in Figure 10(d), ArceKV achieves the highest throughput when evaluated against Pebble, WiredTiger, and Cassandra on Workload III. WiredTiger offers strong write performance but suffers on range lookups due to its size-tiered compaction, which merges up to 15 chunks [67], leading to more sorted runs and higher lookup overhead. Cassandra incurs substantial local overhead from its distributed consistency mechanisms, resulting in lower performance. Pebble performs slightly worse than RocksDB, likely due to Go’s garbage collection and system call costs. In contrast, ArceKV efficiently manages on-disk data and dynamically adjusts write stalls, consistently outperforming these baselines.

**ArceKV delivers strong performance under query-skewed workloads.** To evaluate ArceKV’s ability to handle skewed query distributions, we benchmark it against six alternative methods using the YCSB suite. The workloads include: YCSB-A (Read-Write balanced), YCSB-B (Read-heavy), YCSB-C (Read-only), YCSB-D (Read-heavy with latest keys), YCSB-E (Range-heavy with latest keys), and YCSB-F (Read-Update balanced). Among them, workloads YCSB-C and YCSB-B follow a Zipfian distribution, YCSB-D focuses on the most recent insertions, while YCSB-A, YCSB-E, and YCSB-F use a uniform distribution. Across all workloads, ArceKV consistently achieves top-tier performance.

ArceKV performs exceptionally well in write-read mixed workloads because it avoids re-simulating parameter configurations when the workload shifts, resulting in consistent and robust performance. Moreover, by broadening the compaction space through ElasticLSM, ArceKV is able to identify more effective actions to optimize system behavior.

**Table 3: Performance comparison under diverse query distribution workloads in the YCSB benchmark (normalized throughput)**

| Method | YCSB-A | YCSB-B | YCSB-C | YCSB-D | YCSB-E | YCSB-F |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1-L | 1.44× | 1.00× | 1.94× | 1.00× | 3.89× | 1.42× |
| Lvl | 1.00× | 1.25× | 1.53× | 1.13× | 2.79× | 1.00× |
| Tier | 2.77× | 1.25× | 1.04× | 1.14× | 1.00× | 2.77× |
| LL | 2.11× | 1.29× | 1.00× | 1.24× | 1.07× | 2.17× |
| MSE | 1.87× | 1.84× | 1.62× | 1.57× | 2.89× | 1.89× |
| RKY | 2.42× | 2.05× | 1.55× | 1.25× | 1.45× | 2.72× |
| CAM | 1.37× | 2.03× | 1.49× | 1.60× | 2.45× | 1.37× |
| OURS | 4.18× | 2.26× | 2.34× | 1.89× | 5.47× | 4.21× |

**Figure 10: (a) and (b) illustrate the stabilization speed of ArceKV; (c) reports its performance under multi-threaded workloads; and (d) compares ArceKV with other industrial key-value stores. The table on the right summarizes ArceKV’s performance under skewed workloads (e.g., YCSB). Throughputs (operations per second) of all systems are normalized relative to the minimum value observed in each workload.**
sections.translationMarkdown:
**总体而言，ArceKV 在 Workload I 和 Workload II 下始终表现强劲。**图 8 在两个长期运行的负载 Workload I 和 Workload II 上比较了七种方法；每个负载由多个具有不同读写比例的子负载组成，数据总量最高达到 140GiB。在两个负载中，ArceKV 始终维持顶尖性能，除从负载 B 转换到负载 D 的阶段外，几乎在所有子负载中都排名第一。这一转换会从极端写密集型负载 B 突然切换到读密集型负载 D，对系统的快速适应能力构成很大挑战。尽管变化突兀，ArceKV 仍具有竞争力，只略微落后于面向读优化的两个基线 1-Leveling 和 Leveling。在 Workload II 中，从 F 到 D 的转换没有这么剧烈，此时 ArceKV 与 1-Leveling 和 Leveling 的性能相近。这两个基线针对读密集型负载进行了优化，却会因频繁且激进的 compaction 而在写密集型场景中表现不佳。相反，Tiering 和 LazyLeveling 允许每层存在更多 sorted run，并采用更惰性的 compaction 策略，能够减少写停顿并在写密集型负载下表现良好，但过多 run 的累积会损害读取性能。

Moose 没有为不同负载间的结构配置适应提供稳健方案。它针对不同负载生成的配置差异很大（例如大小比率从 7,7,7,6,5 变为 24,23,24），若不造成严重性能下降，就无法直接在这些配置间转换。为了缓解该问题，我们根据插入 entry 的总数和平均负载组成，为 Moose 计算统一配置。同样，由于 CAMAL 也面向静态负载设计，我们把平均负载组成提供给其训练好的模型，以推导结构参数。在这一设置下，CAMAL 和 Moose 的总体性能都不错，但在 B 和 E 等极端负载中会显著恶化，远远落后于表现最好的基线。相比之下，Ruskey 会动态计算最合适的配置并采用高效适应策略。在负载逐渐变化或 update 数量充足的场景中，Ruskey 表现令人满意；但在突变或读密集型负载下，它的适应过程可能滞后。如图 9(a) 和 (b) 所示，在 update 充足的负载 F 下，Ruskey 的收敛速度与 ArceKV 相当；但在转换至 D 等高度读密集型负载时，其适应速度更慢。

此外，如图 8(c) 和 (d) 所示，与其他方法相比，ArceKV 能随时间始终维持较低的 P99.9 延迟，且不会产生显著的写停顿或读取开销。这说明 ArceKV 在变化的负载条件下具有稳健性和稳定性。

**图 8：所有方法在 Workload I 和 Workload II 下的平均延迟与 P99.9 延迟。**

**ArceKV 通过灵活的 LSM 结构 ElasticLSM，更智能地协调 compaction 和停顿。** 图 9 给出了 Workload I 的更多指标，以突出 ElasticLSM 结构灵活性的收益。在 A 和 D 等读密集型负载下，ElasticLSM 能通过执行多层 compaction，甚至比 Leveling 更快地减少 sorted run 数量。相反，在 B 等写密集型负载下，ElasticLSM 允许系统累积更多 sorted run，从而比 Tiering 和 LazyLeveling 更积极地推迟 compaction。这使 ArceKV 能在维持较低写停顿时间的同时，必要时又像 Leveling 一样积极执行 compaction。因此，长期来看 ArceKV 能取得几乎与 1-Leveling 相同的读 I/O 效率，体现出它在 compaction 激进程度与写停顿控制之间动态平衡的能力。

**图 9：左侧表格给出 Workload I 和 Workload II 中各子负载的归一化吞吐量；右侧各图给出执行 Workload I 时写停顿、compaction 总字节数、sorted run 数量以及读 I/O 总量的变化。**

**ArceKV 在并发负载下展现出优于其他基线的可扩展性。** 如图 10(c) 所示，我们在 1、4、8 和 16 个前台线程下，使用 Workload III 评估六种方法（不包括 Ruskey 和 CAMAL）。之所以排除 Ruskey 和 CAMAL，是因为它们依赖收集系统性能指标，却缺少多线程场景所需的同步机制。此外，Moose 框架不支持多个后台 worker，因此我们将后台线程数固定为 1，只改变前台查询线程数。总体上，线程数增加时所有方法的吞吐量都有提升，但 ArceKV 的增幅最大。这是因为线程数越多，update 越密集，sorted run 的累积也越快。1-Leveling 和 Leveling 等方法由于停顿阈值低，会受困于频繁写停顿；Tiering 和 LazyLeveling 因阈值更高而表现较好。相比之下，ArceKV 会考虑多线程因素，动态调整停顿阈值和 compaction 调度（第 §4 节），从而有效平衡读写性能并取得最佳可扩展性。

**ArceKV 优于其他工业级键值存储。** 如图 10(d) 所示，在 Workload III 上与 Pebble、WiredTiger 和 Cassandra 比较时，ArceKV 的吞吐量最高。WiredTiger 写性能强，但其 size-tiered compaction 最多会合并 15 个 chunk [67]，由此产生更多 sorted run 和更高的 lookup 开销，导致 range lookup 性能不佳。Cassandra 的分布式一致性机制带来大量本地开销，因而性能较低。Pebble 的性能略差于 RocksDB，可能源于 Go 的垃圾回收和系统调用开销。相比之下，ArceKV 高效管理磁盘数据并动态调整写停顿，因此始终优于这些基线。

**ArceKV 在查询偏斜负载下也表现强劲。** 为评估 ArceKV 处理偏斜查询分布的能力，我们使用 YCSB 套件，将它与另外六种方法进行比较。负载包括 YCSB-A（读写均衡）、YCSB-B（读密集）、YCSB-C（只读）、YCSB-D（偏向最新 key 的读密集负载）、YCSB-E（偏向最新 key 的 range 密集负载）和 YCSB-F（read-update 均衡）。其中，YCSB-C 和 YCSB-B 服从 Zipf 分布，YCSB-D 聚焦最近插入的数据，而 YCSB-A、YCSB-E 和 YCSB-F 使用均匀分布。在所有负载上，ArceKV 始终取得顶尖性能。

ArceKV 在读写混合负载中尤其出色，因为负载变化时它无需重新模拟参数配置，从而获得一致而稳健的性能。此外，ElasticLSM 扩大了 compaction 空间，使 ArceKV 能识别更有效的动作来优化系统行为。

**表 3：YCSB benchmark 中不同查询分布负载下的性能比较（归一化吞吐量）**

| 方法 | YCSB-A | YCSB-B | YCSB-C | YCSB-D | YCSB-E | YCSB-F |
|---|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|---:|
| 1-L | 1.44× | 1.00× | 1.94× | 1.00× | 3.89× | 1.42× |
| Lvl | 1.00× | 1.25× | 1.53× | 1.13× | 2.79× | 1.00× |
| Tier | 2.77× | 1.25× | 1.04× | 1.14× | 1.00× | 2.77× |
| LL | 2.11× | 1.29× | 1.00× | 1.24× | 1.07× | 2.17× |
| MSE | 1.87× | 1.84× | 1.62× | 1.57× | 2.89× | 1.89× |
| RKY | 2.42× | 2.05× | 1.55× | 1.25× | 1.45× | 2.72× |
| CAM | 1.37× | 2.03× | 1.49× | 1.60× | 2.45× | 1.37× |
| OURS | 4.18× | 2.26× | 2.34× | 1.89× | 5.47× | 4.21× |

**图 10：(a) 和 (b) 展示 ArceKV 的稳定速度；(c) 报告其在多线程负载下的性能；(d) 将 ArceKV 与其他工业级键值存储进行比较。右侧表格总结 ArceKV 在偏斜负载（例如 YCSB）下的性能。所有系统的吞吐量（每秒操作数）都相对于各负载中观测到的最小值进行归一化。**

sections.title: 5.2 Parameter Studies
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Recomputing Threshold $d$.** Figure 11(a) shows the background cost and foreground query latency under different values of the recomputing threshold $d$, which determines how much the tree state or workload must change before recomputing the parameter tuple $(M,c,k)$. When $d$ is too small, parameters are recomputed too frequently, resulting in substantial background overhead that may slightly impact foreground query latency. As $d$ increases, the background cost drops sharply and stabilizes beyond $d=0.1$, where query latency also reaches its lowest point. This suggests that $d=0.1$ offers a good balance between minimizing recomputation cost and maintaining responsiveness to dynamic workload changes.

**Simulation MaxIterTime.** This parameter controls the number of iterations (i.e., compaction decisions) used to evaluate the effectiveness of a given parameter tuple $(M,c,k)$ under the current workload and tree state in the simulation. As shown in Figure 11(b), setting MaxIterTime too high can prolong the simulation and delay the timely adaptation of parameters, potentially degrading performance. Conversely, setting it too low may fail to capture the long-term effects of compactions, leading to suboptimal parameter choices. Through empirical evaluation, we find that 400 iterations offer a good balance between responsiveness and evaluation accuracy.

**Justification of ArceKV’s Cost Model.** To evaluate the accuracy of the cost model proposed in Section §3, we compare the model’s predictions against actual system latency during the execution of sub-workload J in Workload II. As shown in Figure 11(c), the predicted cost closely follows the trend of the observed latency over time. For each operation, we decompose both the theoretical and real costs into three categories: point lookups, range lookups, and updates. The results indicate that the estimated cost for each operation type aligns well with the actual measured latency, validating the effectiveness of our model.

**Accuracy of Estimating Compaction Windows.** In Section §3.2, we estimate the number of count windows spanned by a compaction using Equation 5. Figure 11(d) presents over 500 estimated compaction durations, ranging from 4MB to nearly 20GB, compared against their actual elapsed windows when running sub-workload J in Workload II. The results indicate that while estimation accuracy decreases slightly for larger compactions with longer execution times, the absolute error remains bounded within 3 windows.

**Figure 11: Internal parameter studies of ArceKV**

**Common Parameter Studies of LSM-Trees.** To assess the robustness of our method, we evaluate ArceKV using Workload III under a variety of commonly used LSM-tree configurations. As these parameters are tuned by CAMAL’s model, we exclude it from this evaluation. These include buffer sizes ranging from 16MB to 128MB, cache sizes from 128MB to 4096MB, and entry sizes from 128B to 2048B, presented in Figure 12(a) to (c). Across all 12 tested configurations, ArceKV consistently delivers the strongest or near-best performance, demonstrating its adaptability and resilience to diverse system settings.

**Space Amplification.** Although ArceKV focuses on read and write performance rather than space efficiency, its space amplification remains well controlled, as shown in Figure 12(d). Under workload J, which includes 36GiB of new updates and 18GiB of duplicates, Leveling yields the lowest amplification, but ArceKV follows closely with only a 0.05 increase. This is because ArceKV’s lookup-driven compactions still merge overlapping runs, effectively removing duplicates and limiting space growth.

**Background Cost in ArceKV.** Figure 12(e) shows the CPU time spent on background tasks in ArceKV, including compaction, flushing, simulation, and decision-making. With suitable values for $d$ and MaxIterTime, simulation adds only 2% overhead, and decision-making contributes less than 1%, due to the efficiency of score-based selection and SIMD acceleration.

**Figure 12: Common LSM parameter studies**
sections.translationMarkdown:
**重新计算阈值 $d$。** 图 11(a) 展示重新计算阈值 $d$ 取不同值时的后台开销与前台查询延迟。该阈值决定树状态或负载需要发生多大变化，系统才会重新计算参数元组 $(M,c,k)$。当 $d$ 过小时，参数重新计算得过于频繁，会产生大量后台开销，并可能轻微影响前台查询延迟。随着 $d$ 增大，后台开销迅速下降，并在 $d=0.1$ 之后趋于稳定；查询延迟也在此处达到最低。这表明 $d=0.1$ 能在降低重新计算开销与维持对动态负载变化的响应能力之间取得良好平衡。

**模拟 MaxIterTime。** 该参数控制模拟中用于评估给定参数元组 $(M,c,k)$ 在当前负载和树状态下有效性的迭代次数（即 compaction 决策次数）。如图 11(b) 所示，MaxIterTime 设置得过高会延长模拟时间，推迟参数的及时适应，并可能降低性能。相反，设置得过低又可能无法捕捉 compaction 的长期影响，导致参数选择次优。通过经验评估，我们发现 400 次迭代在响应能力和评估准确性之间取得了良好平衡。

**ArceKV 代价模型的合理性。** 为评估第 §3 节所提出代价模型的准确性，我们在执行 Workload II 的子负载 J 时，将模型预测与真实系统延迟进行比较。如图 11(c) 所示，预测代价紧密跟随观测延迟随时间变化的趋势。对于每种操作，我们都将理论代价和真实代价分解为 point lookup、range lookup 和 update 三类。结果显示，每种操作的估计代价都与实际测得的延迟较为吻合，验证了模型的有效性。

**Compaction 窗口估计的准确性。** 在第 §3.2 节中，我们使用公式 5 估计一次 compaction 跨越的计数窗口数量。图 11(d) 给出了 500 多次 compaction 的估计持续时间，其大小从 4MB 到接近 20GB，并与执行 Workload II 子负载 J 时实际经过的窗口数进行比较。结果表明，尽管对更大、执行时间更长的 compaction，估计准确度会略微下降，但绝对误差始终不超过 3 个窗口。

**图 11：ArceKV 内部参数研究**

**LSM-tree 通用参数研究。** 为评估方法的稳健性，我们在多种常用 LSM-tree 配置下使用 Workload III 评估 ArceKV。由于 CAMAL 的模型会调优这些参数，因此本项评估排除 CAMAL。测试参数包括 16MB 到 128MB 的 buffer 大小、128MB 到 4096MB 的 cache 大小，以及 128B 到 2048B 的 entry 大小，如图 12(a) 至 (c) 所示。在全部 12 种配置中，ArceKV 始终取得最强或接近最强的性能，说明它能适应不同系统设置并保持稳健。

**空间放大。** 尽管 ArceKV 关注读写性能而非空间效率，其空间放大仍得到良好控制，如图 12(d) 所示。在包含 36GiB 新 update 和 18GiB 重复数据的负载 J 下，Leveling 的放大最低，而 ArceKV 仅比它高 0.05，紧随其后。这是因为 ArceKV 由 lookup 驱动的 compaction 仍会合并相互重叠的 run，从而有效删除重复数据并限制空间增长。

**ArceKV 的后台开销。** 图 12(e) 给出 ArceKV 后台任务消耗的 CPU 时间，包括 compaction、flush、模拟和决策。在为 $d$ 和 MaxIterTime 选取适当值后，模拟仅增加 2% 的开销；得益于基于评分的高效选择与 SIMD 加速，决策开销低于 1%。

**图 12：通用 LSM 参数研究**

sections.title: 5.3 Case Studies
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Read Amplification under MVCC.** When updates concentrate on a small key set, write amplification stays low but long version chains can hurt read performance. To evaluate ArceKV under this condition, we simulate a multi-version workload with 1 million hot keys over 100 million operations, following Workload III (updates, point, and range lookups). We report average latency and read amplification, as requested by the reviewer, in Figure 13(a) and (b). In theory, Leveling and 1-Leveling are well suited to this setting due to the small size increase (1 GiB) and aggressive pruning. Similarly, ElasticLSM detects small incoming runs, assigns low compaction penalties (Equation 11), and converges to Leveling-like behavior, achieving comparable read latency and amplification.

**Continuously Changing Workload.** We gradually vary the write ratio from 10% to 90% and back to 10%, creating a smooth workload gradient rather than abrupt changes. At each ratio, we issue about 500,000 mixed read and write operations to reflect realistic fluctuations. As shown in Figure 13(c) and (d), ArceKV achieves the best or near-best performance throughout execution and the lowest average latency. These results demonstrate that ArceKV maintains stable, adaptive performance under smoothly evolving workloads, enabled by its workload-aware compaction and write-stall mechanisms.

**ArceKV with Range Filters.** As shown in Figure 13(e) and (f), to assess how range filters [16, 21, 83] enhance ArceKV, we integrate Grafite [21], a lightweight and easily pluggable range filter, and compare it with GRF [83], a state-of-the-art LSM-tree system featuring range filtering. Grafite in ArceKV is serialized into SSTables during compaction and deserialized during queries. Additionally, we record the effective ratio of the filters (defined as the reduction in run accesses divided by the total number of run accesses) and multiply this ratio by the cost of range lookups. We evaluate mixed workloads with update ratios from 10% to 90%. Overall, ArceKV+Grafite improves throughput by about 20% over the original version and achieves roughly half the latency of GRF. The gap arises because GRF’s LazyLeveling policy improves point lookups and updates but adapts poorly to shifting update ratios, whereas its advanced position encoding gives it an edge in pure range lookups. Incorporating such encoding into ArceKV remains a direction for future work.

**Figure 13: Case studies of various cases: (a) and (b) are the latencies of long multi-versioned chain scenario; (c) and (d) are latencies of continuous workloads; (e) and (f) are latency of ArceKV integrated with range filter.**
sections.translationMarkdown:
**MVCC 下的读放大。** 当 update 集中于较小的 key 集合时，写放大会保持较低，但较长的版本链会损害读取性能。为了评估 ArceKV 在这种条件下的表现，我们依照 Workload III（包含 update、point lookup 和 range lookup），在 1 亿次操作上模拟具有 100 万个热点 key 的多版本负载。应审稿人要求，我们在图 13(a) 和 (b) 中报告平均延迟和读放大。从理论上看，由于数据量增长较小（1 GiB）且会积极剪枝，Leveling 和 1-Leveling 很适合这一场景。类似地，ElasticLSM 会检测较小的新到达 run，为其分配较低的 compaction 惩罚（公式 11），并收敛到类似 Leveling 的行为，从而取得相当的读取延迟与放大。

**连续变化的工作负载。** 我们让写比例从 10% 逐渐提高到 90%，再逐渐降回 10%，构造平滑的负载梯度而不是突变。在每个比例下，发出约 500,000 次混合读写操作，以反映真实波动。如图 13(c) 和 (d) 所示，ArceKV 在整个执行期间取得最佳或接近最佳的性能，并具有最低平均延迟。这些结果表明，凭借工作负载感知的 compaction 和写停顿机制，ArceKV 能在平滑演变的负载下保持稳定且自适应的性能。

**结合 Range Filter 的 ArceKV。** 如图 13(e) 和 (f) 所示，为评估 range filter [16, 21, 83] 对 ArceKV 的增强效果，我们集成了轻量且易于插接的 range filter Grafite [21]，并与采用 range filtering 的先进 LSM-tree 系统 GRF [83] 进行比较。ArceKV 中的 Grafite 在 compaction 期间被序列化到 SSTable，并在查询期间反序列化。此外，我们记录 filter 的有效比例（定义为 run 访问减少量除以 run 访问总量），并将该比例乘以 range lookup 的代价。我们评估 update 比例从 10% 到 90% 的混合负载。总体上，ArceKV+Grafite 相比原始版本将吞吐量提高约 20%，且延迟约为 GRF 的一半。出现差距的原因是，GRF 的 LazyLeveling 策略虽然改善了 point lookup 和 update，却无法很好地适应变化的 update 比例；另一方面，它先进的位置编码使其在纯 range lookup 中占优。将这种编码引入 ArceKV 仍是未来工作方向。

**图 13：多种场景的案例研究：(a) 和 (b) 为长多版本链场景的延迟；(c) 和 (d) 为连续变化负载的延迟；(e) 和 (f) 为集成 range filter 后 ArceKV 的延迟。**

sections.title: 6 RELATED WORK
sections.originalMarkdown:
**Key-value stores.** Over the past decade, extensive research has advanced key-value stores. Hardware-focused studies [9, 32, 54, 82, 85, 92, 93, 102] optimize for modern storage technologies, including advanced SSDs [17, 32, 92, 93, 99], RDMA [86, 88], non-volatile memory [48, 54, 103, 107], and disaggregated memory architectures [40, 77, 91]. These systems improve parallelism [85] and write throughput [9, 82, 102] by aligning architectures with hardware capabilities. In cloud environments, several works [45, 49, 79] integrate cloud-specific overheads into system design, while others rethink key-value store architectures entirely [3, 55, 63, 88, 101, 110], enabling new system-level innovations. Unlike our work, these efforts target diverse environments and architectures, rather than focusing specifically on LSM-tree optimization.

**Optimization of LSM-based key-value stores.** There has been rapid progress in optimizing LSM-trees, driven by rethinking their core components. Innovations include advanced compaction policies [14, 26, 27, 43, 44, 46, 58, 66, 74, 84, 105] and update-friendly compaction schemes [29, 71, 73, 75, 90, 96, 97], both aiming to balance write amplification and responsiveness. Other work improves filtering structures like Bloom filters [25, 28, 56, 70, 104, 108, 109], range filters [16, 51, 62, 100], and cache policies [87, 89] to cut unnecessary I/O. Additional directions leverage emerging hardware [4, 31, 57, 81, 82, 85, 102], bridge LSM-trees with update-in-place architectures [99], and optimize via key-value separation [23, 24, 59], disaggregated storage [10], selective flushing [7], and improved memory/concurrency management [11, 36, 50, 61, 78]. Other studies target tail-latency reduction [8, 60, 75], exploit data characteristics [2, 65, 72, 94], or adapt LSM-trees to cloud environments [14, 15, 42]. Most, however, optimize LSM-based key-value stores without explicitly incorporating workload characteristics into their design.

**Cost-Model-Based Compaction Optimization.** Unlike traditional Leveling and Tiering policies, recent LSM-tree optimization techniques employ cost models that quantify the cost of different operations to identify the most efficient compaction strategy [26, 27, 58, 64, 66, 84, 98]. These approaches can be broadly classified into two categories: workload-unaware and workload-aware methods. The former seek generally robust performance across diverse workloads, whereas the latter aim to achieve optimal efficiency for specific workload patterns. Workload-unaware methods such as LazyLeveling [26], EcoTune [84], Moose [58], Spooky [29], and QLSM-Bush [27] model the costs of various operations and theoretically determine structural parameters or compaction behaviors that provide stable performance across diverse workloads. In contrast, workload-aware approaches such as Vertiorizon [66], CAMAL [98], Endure [43], and SMoose [58], explicitly incorporate the operation probabilities of a given workload into their cost models, enabling more accurate parameter tuning for targeted scenarios. However, while workload awareness improves specialization, adapting between parameter configurations as workloads shift can incur substantial transition costs. Ruskey [64] addresses this issue using reinforcement learning to mitigate transition inefficiencies. By contrast, ArceKV not only considers workload composition but also explicitly models and optimizes the transition process itself, enabling continuous performance optimization rather than discrete parameter transitioning.
sections.translationMarkdown:
**键值存储。** 过去十年间，大量研究推动了键值存储的发展。面向硬件的研究 [9, 32, 54, 82, 85, 92, 93, 102] 针对现代存储技术进行优化，包括先进 SSD [17, 32, 92, 93, 99]、RDMA [86, 88]、非易失性内存 [48, 54, 103, 107] 和解耦内存架构 [40, 77, 91]。这些系统通过使体系结构与硬件能力相匹配，提高并行性 [85] 和写吞吐量 [9, 82, 102]。在云环境中，一些工作 [45, 49, 79] 将云特有的开销纳入系统设计，另一些工作则彻底重新思考键值存储架构 [3, 55, 63, 88, 101, 110]，从而实现新的系统级创新。与本文不同，这些工作面向多种环境和体系结构，而非专门关注 LSM-tree 优化。

**基于 LSM 的键值存储优化。** 通过重新思考 LSM-tree 的核心组件，相关优化已快速发展。创新包括先进的 compaction 策略 [14, 26, 27, 43, 44, 46, 58, 66, 74, 84, 105] 和对 update 友好的 compaction 方案 [29, 71, 73, 75, 90, 96, 97]，两者都旨在平衡写放大与响应能力。其他工作改进 Bloom filter [25, 28, 56, 70, 104, 108, 109]、range filter [16, 51, 62, 100] 等过滤结构以及 cache 策略 [87, 89]，以减少不必要的 I/O。其他方向利用新兴硬件 [4, 31, 57, 81, 82, 85, 102]，将 LSM-tree 与原地更新架构结合 [99]，并通过 KV 分离 [23, 24, 59]、存储解耦 [10]、选择性 flush [7] 以及改进的内存/并发管理 [11, 36, 50, 61, 78] 进行优化。另一些研究则致力于降低尾延迟 [8, 60, 75]、利用数据特征 [2, 65, 72, 94]，或让 LSM-tree 适应云环境 [14, 15, 42]。然而，其中大多数工作优化基于 LSM 的键值存储时，并未明确将负载特征纳入设计。

**基于代价模型的 Compaction 优化。** 与传统 Leveling 和 Tiering 策略不同，近期的 LSM-tree 优化技术使用代价模型量化不同操作的代价，以识别最高效的 compaction 策略 [26, 27, 58, 64, 66, 84, 98]。这些方法大体可分为两类：工作负载无关方法和工作负载感知方法。前者寻求在多种负载上普遍稳健的性能，后者则力求针对特定负载模式取得最优效率。LazyLeveling [26]、EcoTune [84]、Moose [58]、Spooky [29] 和 QLSM-Bush [27] 等工作负载无关方法对各种操作的代价进行建模，并从理论上确定可在不同负载下提供稳定性能的结构参数或 compaction 行为。相比之下，Vertiorizon [66]、CAMAL [98]、Endure [43] 和 SMoose [58] 等工作负载感知方法明确把给定负载的操作概率纳入代价模型，从而能针对目标场景进行更准确的参数调优。然而，工作负载感知虽然提高了针对性，当负载变化时在参数配置之间适应仍可能产生大量转换开销。Ruskey [64] 使用强化学习缓解转换低效问题。相比之下，ArceKV 不仅考虑负载组成，还明确建模并优化转换过程本身，从而实现连续性能优化，而非离散的参数转换。

sections.title: 7 CONCLUSION
sections.originalMarkdown: Existing LSM-based key-value stores perform poorly under dynamic workloads due to static compaction and write-stall policies. We propose ArceKV, a workload-driven system that adaptively schedules compactions and adjusts write-stall thresholds based on the current workload and LSM-tree state. Experiments show that ArceKV consistently outperforms state-of-the-art approaches under evolving workloads.
sections.translationMarkdown: 现有基于 LSM 的键值存储由于采用静态 compaction 与写停顿策略，在动态工作负载下表现不佳。我们提出 ArceKV：一种工作负载驱动的系统，它根据当前负载和 LSM-tree 状态，自适应地调度 compaction 并调整写停顿阈值。实验表明，在持续演变的负载下，ArceKV 始终优于现有先进方法。
